ocr.text: In the context of evaluating the diverse training backgrounds that contribute to the understanding of immune system
modulation, the data presented in the table serves as a critical resource. The responses to the question "What training do you
have?" highlight the varying degrees of formal education, work experience, and life experience among participants. Notably, the
metrics of "First response" and "Receptor" provide insight into how different educational and experiential backgrounds
influence knowledge of specific biochemical pathways and their associated functions. For example, respondents with formal
education in immunology may demonstrate a more comprehensive understanding of the role of receptors like PAR-1 and
signaling pathways involving CCL2, PKC/c-SRC/EGFR/MEK/ERK/AP-1. This knowledge is crucial for elucidating the
activation mechanisms of monocytes, memory T cells, and dendritic cells within various therapeutic contexts. Understanding
these relationships not only enhances academic discourse but also informs treatment strategies aimed at optimizing immune
responses. The findings underscore the importance of diverse training in advancing both research and clinical applications in
immunotherapy.
Questions  First response  Second response  Third response
What training do you have?  Formal education  Work experience  Life experience
What attributes make you effective?  Communication  Empathy  Flexibility and personal awareness
In what ways do you feel supported?  Financially  Socially  Mentally and emotionally
What resources are available to you?  Work space  Training  Case consultation
Who do you collaborate with?  Colleagues  Health care providers  Social services
Receptor  Regulation  Pathway  Function  Reference
PAR-1  CCL2  PKC/c-SRC/EGFR/MEK/ERK/AP-1  Monocytes, memory T cells, and dendritic cells activation  [47, 48]
PAR-1/-2  IL-1, TNF-α  p38/p42/44  MMP expression  [65]
PAR-1/-3  HO-1  PKCδ/c-SRC/Nrf-2  Synovial fibroblasts inflammatory  [70–72]
PAR-1/-3  MMP-13  PKCδ/c-SRC/EGFR/pI3K/AKT/AP-1  Cartilage destruction  [59, 60]
PAR-1  COX-2  ?  IL-6, CTGF, FGF1/2, VEGF, Tnc expression  [51–54]
